answers:
**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a config/data desync, not a model or classifier problem. `ticket-scorer/pipeline/score_batch.py` reads the name of the gold-label field to compare against from `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field` key, then does `gold = record.get(gold_field)`. The eval-set records in `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` store the gold label under the key `"gold_label"`. But `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` still says `expected_label_field: label` — a key that doesn't exist in any record. So `record.get("label")` returns `None` for every single record, `predict_category(...)` never returns `None` (it always returns one of `billing`/`bug`/`account`/`shipping`/`other`), so `prediction == gold` is `False` for every record, and `statistics.mean(hits)` is exactly `0.0`.

The file that needs to change is **`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`**: `expected_label_field` should be `gold_label`, not `label`. This is a one-line config fix, not a code or model fix. (I verified this directly: when I compared predictions against `gold_label` instead of the configured `label`, 10 of 11 eval records matched, giving 0.909 — consistent with the historical ~0.91 baseline in the metrics log.)

**2. When it began and what introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, introduced by commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"). That commit renamed the gold-label key in every record of `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label`, and updated `score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` set to match (`{"text", "label"}` → `{"text", "gold_label"}`) — but it never updated `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`, which was set once in the original `SCORE-41` commit (2026-05-20) and never touched again (confirmed via `git log --follow -p` on that file). The recorded nightly accuracy log (`pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv`) shows `2026-06-16,0.91` immediately followed by `2026-06-17,0.00` and every date after that through `2026-07-06,0.00` — an exact match to both the commit date and the "about three weeks ago" symptom description (2026-06-17 to 2026-07-12 is ~3.5 weeks).

**3. Do I agree with "not reproducible / flaky"? No.**
This is not flaky — it is 100% deterministic and reproduces on every run, which I confirmed directly (`python3 -c "...score()"` → `0` every time). There is no model call and no randomness anywhere in this pipeline: `classifier.py`'s docstring states it explicitly ("No network and no model call... fully reproducible"), and the classifier is a pure keyword-matching function.

The prior investigation's error was testing the wrong code path. `pipeline/score_one.py` (used for the spot-checks) calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and just prints the label — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads a gold label, and never computes accuracy at all. So re-running tickets through it can only ever confirm that the classifier's predictions look sane; it cannot possibly surface a bug that lives entirely in the batch scorer's config-driven gold-field lookup. The predictions "looking correct" by hand was never in question — the eval-set comparison logic was broken, and single-item runs structurally cannot exercise that logic.

The correct way to reproduce: run the actual nightly entry point, `pipeline/score_batch.py` (i.e., call `score()` or run the module, which I did), against the real eval set and config — not `score_one.py`. Doing so reproduces `accuracy = 0.0` immediately and every time, with no variance across repeated runs. A single `print(record.get(gold_field))` or a debugger breakpoint inside `score()` would have shown `gold` is `None` for every record within seconds.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step ("switch the nightly runner back to the previous model").**
I would not act on this. It targets the wrong layer and won't fix anything, for two independent reasons: (a) there is no model to switch — this classifier is an explicitly deterministic, network-free keyword-matching stub (confirmed by reading `classifier.py`), so "the previous model" isn't a variable that could have caused this at all; and (b) even setting that aside, the actual defect is a config/data mismatch (`expected_label_field: label` vs. the eval set's `gold_label` key) that has nothing to do with which model or model version is used for predictions — reverting a "model" would leave `config/scoring_config.yaml` unchanged and accuracy would stay at exactly 0.0. Pursuing this step would burn time/risk on an unrelated rollback while leaving the real one-line config bug in place indefinitely.
report:
## Plan

The task describes a nightly LLM pipeline that classifies support tickets and scores against a labeled eval set, with accuracy dropping from ~0.91 to 0.0 about three weeks ago and staying there, individual predictions looking fine, and a prior investigation closed as "not reproducible / flaky." Per the systematic-debugging-playbook (read first, in full), I searched for prior art before hypothesizing, treated the existing "not reproducible" investigation as needing re-verification rather than trust, and aimed to reproduce at the lowest layer that shows the bug rather than guessing. I located the matching project (`ticket-scorer` — nightly LLM ticket classifier scored against a labeled eval set, SCORE-N ticket scheme) among several superficially similar sibling projects in the repo, then traced its batch-scoring code path, its git history, and its recorded nightly metrics to find the exact defect and the exact commit that introduced it, and reproduced the failure directly.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/systematic-debugging-playbook/SKILL.md` in full (guide for this investigation) — establishes: search prior art first, don't trust a "not reproducible" classification without re-verifying via the real trigger path, reproduce at the lowest layer that shows the bug.
2. Ran `find . -maxdepth 3` to survey the repo — found many similarly-named LLM-scoring projects (`answer-scoring-pipeline`, `batch-grader`, `outputs-review`, `scoreforge`, `tallgrass`, `reply-grader`, etc.). Identified `ticket-scorer` as the one matching the task ("classifies incoming support tickets," "SCORE-N," has an `investigations/` folder with a note dated 2026-06-20).
3. Read `ticket-scorer/README.md` — confirms: nightly LLM ticket-classification pipeline, deterministic offline keyword-stub classifier (no network, no model, so batch/single-item runs are fully reproducible — this is a load-bearing fact).
4. Read `ticket-scorer/investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` — the prior note: accuracy dropped ~0.91→~0.0 around 2026-06-17, engineer re-ran ~20 failing tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py` one at a time, all looked correct, closed as "not reproducible / flaky," suggested reverting the nightly runner to "the previous model."
5. Read `ticket-scorer/pipeline/score_batch.py`, `score_one.py`, `classifier.py`, `preprocess.py` — found `score_batch.score()` reads `expected_label_field` from `config/scoring_config.yaml`, does `gold = record.get(gold_field)`, and compares `prediction == gold`; `score_one.py` never touches config or gold labels at all — it just prints `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))`.
6. Read `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` — `expected_label_field: label`.
7. Read `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` — every record's gold key is `"gold_label"`, not `"label"`.
8. Ran `git log --oneline -20` and `git log --format='%H %ad %s' --date=iso` inside `ticket-scorer/` (it's its own git repo) — 5 commits total.
9. Ran `git show 53551e8` (`chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)`, 2026-06-17) — this commit renamed every eval record's key from `label` to `gold_label` and updated `score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}`, but **did not touch `config/scoring_config.yaml`**, which still says `expected_label_field: label`.
10. Ran `git log --follow -p -- config/scoring_config.yaml` — the file was created once, in the original `SCORE-41` commit (2026-05-20), with `expected_label_field: label`, and was never modified afterward, confirming the 06-17 migration commit is what desynchronized it from the data.
11. Ran `git show e96cd06` and `git show 8917ba8` (both `chore(metrics): nightly score log`) and inspected `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` — accuracy log reads `...,2026-06-16,0.91` then `2026-06-17,0.00`, `2026-06-18,0.00`, `2026-06-19,0.00`, continuing at `0.00` through the latest recorded run `2026-07-06,0.00`.
12. Searched for any test file covering `score_batch.py` (`find`/`grep` for `test_*.py`, `score_batch`, `REQUIRED_KEYS`, `expected_label_field`) — found none; `ticket-scorer` has no `tests/` directory at all, unlike its sibling projects.
13. Ran `python3 -c "...from pipeline.score_batch import score; print(score())"` from inside `ticket-scorer/` — reproduced `accuracy = 0` directly and deterministically on current HEAD.
14. Ran a follow-up script comparing `predict_category` output against both `rec.get('gold_label')` (correct field) and `rec.get('label')` (configured field) for every eval record — 10/11 predictions match `gold_label` (0.909, matching the historical ~0.91 baseline), 1/11 is a genuine model miss ("payment" ticket matches the `billing` keyword rule before `account`), and 0/11 match `label` because that key doesn't exist in any record (`.get` returns `None` for all 11).
15. Noticed the reproduction run created `pipeline/__pycache__/` as a side effect of Python's import system; checked `git status --porcelain`, confirmed it was untracked, and removed it with `rm -rf` to leave the working tree clean (verified again with `git status --porcelain` → empty), since I'm operating strictly read-only and this was an artifact I generated, not pre-existing repo state.

## Answers

**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a config/data desync, not a model or classifier problem. `ticket-scorer/pipeline/score_batch.py` reads the name of the gold-label field to compare against from `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field` key, then does `gold = record.get(gold_field)`. The eval-set records in `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` store the gold label under the key `"gold_label"`. But `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` still says `expected_label_field: label` — a key that doesn't exist in any record. So `record.get("label")` returns `None` for every single record, `predict_category(...)` never returns `None` (it always returns one of `billing`/`bug`/`account`/`shipping`/`other`), so `prediction == gold` is `False` for every record, and `statistics.mean(hits)` is exactly `0.0`.

The file that needs to change is **`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`**: `expected_label_field` should be `gold_label`, not `label`. This is a one-line config fix, not a code or model fix. (I verified this directly: when I compared predictions against `gold_label` instead of the configured `label`, 10 of 11 eval records matched, giving 0.909 — consistent with the historical ~0.91 baseline in the metrics log.)

**2. When it began and what introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, introduced by commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"). That commit renamed the gold-label key in every record of `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label`, and updated `score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` set to match (`{"text", "label"}` → `{"text", "gold_label"}`) — but it never updated `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`, which was set once in the original `SCORE-41` commit (2026-05-20) and never touched again (confirmed via `git log --follow -p` on that file). The recorded nightly accuracy log (`pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv`) shows `2026-06-16,0.91` immediately followed by `2026-06-17,0.00` and every date after that through `2026-07-06,0.00` — an exact match to both the commit date and the "about three weeks ago" symptom description (2026-06-17 to 2026-07-12 is ~3.5 weeks).

**3. Do I agree with "not reproducible / flaky"? No.**
This is not flaky — it is 100% deterministic and reproduces on every run, which I confirmed directly (`python3 -c "...score()"` → `0` every time). There is no model call and no randomness anywhere in this pipeline: `classifier.py`'s docstring states it explicitly ("No network and no model call... fully reproducible"), and the classifier is a pure keyword-matching function.

The prior investigation's error was testing the wrong code path. `pipeline/score_one.py` (used for the spot-checks) calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and just prints the label — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads a gold label, and never computes accuracy at all. So re-running tickets through it can only ever confirm that the classifier's predictions look sane; it cannot possibly surface a bug that lives entirely in the batch scorer's config-driven gold-field lookup. The predictions "looking correct" by hand was never in question — the eval-set comparison logic was broken, and single-item runs structurally cannot exercise that logic.

The correct way to reproduce: run the actual nightly entry point, `pipeline/score_batch.py` (i.e., call `score()` or run the module, which I did), against the real eval set and config — not `score_one.py`. Doing so reproduces `accuracy = 0.0` immediately and every time, with no variance across repeated runs. A single `print(record.get(gold_field))` or a debugger breakpoint inside `score()` would have shown `gold` is `None` for every record within seconds.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step ("switch the nightly runner back to the previous model").**
I would not act on this. It targets the wrong layer and won't fix anything, for two independent reasons: (a) there is no model to switch — this classifier is an explicitly deterministic, network-free keyword-matching stub (confirmed by reading `classifier.py`), so "the previous model" isn't a variable that could have caused this at all; and (b) even setting that aside, the actual defect is a config/data mismatch (`expected_label_field: label` vs. the eval set's `gold_label` key) that has nothing to do with which model or model version is used for predictions — reverting a "model" would leave `config/scoring_config.yaml` unchanged and accuracy would stay at exactly 0.0. Pursuing this step would burn time/risk on an unrelated rollback while leaving the real one-line config bug in place indefinitely.

## Next steps

1. Fix `config/scoring_config.yaml`: change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`.
2. Add a regression test (there is currently no `tests/` directory in `ticket-scorer` at all, unlike sibling projects in this repo) that calls `score_batch.score()` against the real fixture/config pair and asserts accuracy is in a sane range (e.g., >0.8), so a future field-name/schema drift between config and fixtures fails CI instead of silently zeroing the nightly metric for weeks.
3. Add a guard in `load_config`/`score()` — e.g., fail loudly (raise) if `gold_field` isn't present in the first record, rather than silently defaulting to `None` via `.get()` — so this class of bug surfaces as an immediate error instead of a silent 0.0.
4. Prepend a dated correction banner to `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` per the playbook's write-up convention (never silently edit/delete the original), documenting the real root cause and pointing at this new finding, so the "flaky" conclusion doesn't mislead the next person who greps investigation history for this symptom.
5. Backfill/re-annotate `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` for 2026-06-17 through today (or at least annotate it) so the metric history doesn't read as three-plus weeks of a genuinely broken classifier.

## Confidence and gaps

- **High confidence** on root cause (Q1) and the introducing commit (Q2): directly reproduced `accuracy=0.0` from current HEAD, directly showed 10/11 correct matches when using the right field, and directly traced the exact commit/date via `git show`/`git log` with no ambiguity — this isn't inferred, it's observed.
- **High confidence** on the "not reproducible" verdict being wrong (Q3): the classifier is stub/deterministic by the code's own docstring, and I reproduced the failure with zero variance across runs; the structural reason `score_one.py` can't surface this bug is directly visible in its source (it never reads config or gold labels).
- **High confidence** on the "switch models" recommendation being ineffective (Q4), since it's directly falsified by the code (no model exists to switch) and by the fact that the actual defect is orthogonal to model choice.
- **Gap:** I don't know what the real nightly cron/scheduler invocation looks like in production (only the repo-level `score_batch.py` module was available), so I can't confirm the production nightly job calls `score()` exactly this way rather than through some wrapper — though the metrics CSV's exact 0.00 values on 06-17 strongly suggest it does.
- **Gap:** I could not determine who/what authored commit `53551e8` beyond the `Fixture Bot <fixture@example.com>` author field recorded in git — no linked SCORE-77 ticket content was available in this repo to check whether the config update was scoped out of that ticket intentionally or simply missed.
- No repository state was modified during this investigation; the one incidental artifact (a Python `__pycache__` directory created by my reproduction run) was removed, and `git status` is clean.

Prepend a dated correction banner to `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` per the playbook's write-up convention (never silently edit/delete the original), documenting the real root cause and pointing at this new finding, so the "flaky" conclusion doesn't mislead the next person who greps investigation history for this symptom.
5. Backfill/re-annotate `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` for 2026-06-17 through today (or at least annotate it) so the metric history doesn't read as three-plus weeks of a genuinely broken classifier.
sections.plan: The task describes a nightly LLM pipeline that classifies support tickets and scores against a labeled eval set, with accuracy dropping from ~0.91 to 0.0 about three weeks ago and staying there, individual predictions looking fine, and a prior investigation closed as "not reproducible / flaky." Per the systematic-debugging-playbook (read first, in full), I searched for prior art before hypothesizing, treated the existing "not reproducible" investigation as needing re-verification rather than trust, and aimed to reproduce at the lowest layer that shows the bug rather than guessing. I located the matching project (`ticket-scorer` — nightly LLM ticket classifier scored against a labeled eval set, SCORE-N ticket scheme) among several superficially similar sibling projects in the repo, then traced its batch-scoring code path, its git history, and its recorded nightly metrics to find the exact defect and the exact commit that introduced it, and reproduced the failure directly.
